system_prompt:
You are distilling the entire book into a coherent statement of what it teaches.

The user will give you the list of chapter-level summaries. Write a multi-paragraph
distillation (typically 4-8 paragraphs) that captures:

  - the book's overall arc and pedagogical structure
  - the master's characteristic systems, terminology, and moves
  - the rules and prescriptions the book establishes
  - any explicit method or methodology the book teaches

Cite chapters by number when useful (e.g. "Chapter 3 introduces..."). Do not
invent material outside the summaries. The output is the input to Stage 4
which derives structured systems[] from it, so be precise about what the
book actually says, not what guitar pedagogy generically might say.

Output the prose — no preamble, no JSON. Use markdown headings if helpful.
user_prompt:
All chapter summaries follow.

<<<CHAPTER SUMMARIES
## Chapter 1: Chord Fundamentals and Chord Exercises (Lessons 1-5)

# Chapter 1 — Chord Fundamentals and Chord Exercises (Lessons 1-5)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This opening chapter establishes Baker's core polemic and the architectural scaffolding the rest of the book depends on: rhythm strumming is declared obsolete and must be replaced by a systematic chord vocabulary that powers solos, intros, comping, and fills (Replacing strummed chords). Baker prunes guitar's chord universe down to a curated, numerically referenced set of 26 essential chords, dismissing the rest as 'meaningless,' and anchors initial study in two seed keys, G and C, from which all other keys are derived by transposition (The 26 essential chords; Two starting keys). The load-bearing rules introduced here are non-negotiable and recur throughout the method: transposition to every key is mandatory and is what defines mastery (Transposition is mandatory); the student must keep a hand-written music book to retain the work (Keep a music writing book); every new chord shape is internalized by Baker's signature chromatic up-the-neck drill (Chromatic chord exercise); lessons must not be skipped because the chord-numbering system is cumulative (Don't skip lessons); and rhythm comping itself is restricted to a tight six-chord palette (Chords No. 1-6) that suffices across the four foundational keys F, G, A flat, and B flat (Six core rhythm chords; Most important rhythm chords). The chapter also introduces the two-line standard-vs-new substitution format that becomes the dominant pedagogical device of the book (Standard vs. new chords).

## Chapter 2: Melody Chord Progressions, Introductions, Arpeggio and String Bass Styles (Lessons 6-8)

# Chapter 2 — Melody Chord Progressions, Introductions, Arpeggio and String Bass Styles (Lessons 6-8)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 pivots from rhythm comping into melody-chord playing, introductions, and the two characteristic accompaniment textures Baker elevates above plain strumming - arpeggio and walking string-bass (Melody chord progressions; Arpeggio and string bass styles). The transposition mandate from Chapter 1 is operationalised as a concrete manuscript-paper exercise: the student physically rewrites Lesson 4 into F, A flat, and B flat before going further (Transpose Lesson 4 by hand), and the four-key training scope (F, G, A flat, B flat) is preserved while C, D flat, and E flat are deferred. Baker's signature chromatic up-and-down-the-neck drill is reapplied specifically to intros, with physical familiarity treated as the path to fluency (Chromatic intro practice), and the Lesson 6 intro shapes are designated as the canonical melody-chord vocabulary for these four keys (Most important melody chords). The chapter's load-bearing creative claim is combinatorial: a small fixed chord set generates unlimited intros if the student varies rhythm - playing root-then-chord, arpeggiating - and recombines the shapes in as many ways as possible (Vary rhythm to create intros; Combine intros creatively), with the arpeggio and string-bass studies pinned to a specific template, Intro No. 2, so the texture variations have a concrete object to act on (Reworking Intro No. 2).

## Chapter 3: Chords in C/Db/Eb, Harmonic Structure and Chord Progressing (Lessons 9-12)

# Chapter 3 — Chords in C/Db/Eb, Harmonic Structure and Chord Progressing (Lessons 9-12)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 shifts the workspace to a second three-key cluster - C, D flat, and E flat major - and reapplies the chromatic up-and-down-the-neck drill as the default mechanism for absorbing each new chord shape (Review before new keys; Chord exercises up the neck). Its central theoretical contribution is the substitution axiom: any chord on the major mode (Cma7, Cma6, Cma9) can replace a plain major triad, and the same licence extends to minor (Dmi6/7/9) and to a near-unlimited dominant menu including 13, 9, 7#5+9, 13#5+9, 7b5, 11, and 13b9 voicings (Major triad substitution rule; Minor and dominant substitution). Baker frames this aesthetically: static two-bar strumming is 'corny' and lacks color, while motion through adaptable voicings is what makes the music jazz (Avoid strumming for color). The substitution licence is then bounded by a load-bearing constraint - voicings cannot be thrown in anywhere, they must serve an actual progression and its voice-leading (Substitutions need progression) - and the circled-number annotation system over each chord ties every progression diagram back to the Lesson 1 catalog (Chord-number annotation). The chapter also sets explicit study-timescale and drill-load expectations: months per key, twelve repetitions of each example daily (Years to internalize; Daily repetition mandate).

## Chapter 4: Vamps and Fill-ins (Lessons 13-14)

# Chapter 4 — Vamps and Fill-ins (Lessons 13-14)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 introduces vamps and fill-ins and states what is arguably Baker's central architectural claim about jazz-guitar voicings: with the new chords there are only two position sets per key - in C major one set starts at the third position and the other at the eighth - and mastering both sets is what enables fluent transposition between keys (Two position sets per key). The chapter then codifies Baker's signature reharmonisation engine: any dominant-7 chord can be replaced by the minor a fifth above it (G7 by Dmi7, D7 by Ami), with the student required to write out an explicit twelve-row dominant-to-relative-minor chart from C7 through B7 (Tritone-sub via relative minor; Build a dominant/minor chart). A second concrete reference artefact - a scrap book holding all this material - is prescribed alongside the existing manuscript notebook (Build a scrap book). Vamps themselves are defined as a genre-anchored category specific to jump and novelty tunes, taught only in their canonical progressions, and treated as universal targets for the string-bass and arpeggio textures introduced in Lesson 8 (Vamps for jump/novelty tunes; Apply bass/arpeggio to vamps). The chapter closes by specifying the exact seven-key transposition target list - D flat, E flat, F, G, A, B flat - that the C-major vamp study must cover (Transpose vamps to seven keys).

## Chapter 5: Bridges and Last Lesson in Chord Progressions (Lessons 15-16)

# Chapter 5 — Bridges and Last Lesson in Chord Progressions (Lessons 15-16)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 extends the numbered chord catalog past 26 to add chords 27-30 - four open-string-root voicings used for horn-backing fill-ins - and explains why the new vamp work abandons C major for G: the new shapes depend on guitar-friendly open-string roots only available in certain keys (New chords 27-30; Key choice for new vamps). This forces a critical and explicit caveat on the otherwise-universal transposition mandate - open-string vamp shapes are key-specific and must be selected per key rather than uniformly moved (Some shapes don't transpose). Bridges are then introduced as a distinct study category, taught through the same standard-on-top / new-on-bottom two-line format used throughout the book, with the explicit instruction to drill any hard progression until comfortable (Bridges with new chords; Drill through hard progressions). The chapter culminates - and Baker flags Lesson 16 as the capstone, 'the key to all of the other lessons' - in the dominant-as-leading-chord principle: every chord-to-chord connection in jazz can be built by inserting the appropriate dominant between them, organised into the three-category taxonomy of dominant-to-dominant, dominant-to-minor, and dominant-to-major connections (Dominant as leading chord; Three connection categories). The pedagogical end-goal is named explicitly: drilling these chord-connection patterns trains the ear so the student can hear any song and immediately know what reharmonisation to use (Learn to choose by ear).

## Chapter 6: Applying Chords, Introductions and Endings (Lessons 17-18)

# Chapter 6 — Applying Chords, Introductions and Endings (Lessons 17-18)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

This chapter (Lessons 17-18) teaches how to apply the previously-learned chord voicings to real tunes, with the dominant functioning as the universal lead-in chord that governs every voice-leading choice (Dominant as leading chord). Baker reduces all comping situations to three substitution categories — dominant-to-dominant, dominant-to-minor, and dominant-to-major (Three connection categories) — and insists each pattern be written out and worked through Db, Eb, F, G, Ab, and Bb (Transpose to all keys). The characteristic working-guitarist move surfaced here is treating intros and endings as transposable harmonic templates that get layered onto any melody (Intros and endings approach), with the explicit practice loop of pulling sheet music for four or five popular tunes and substituting the new voicings into them (Applying chords to songs). Load-bearing rules introduced: the all-keys mandate extends to intros and endings (Transpose intros and endings); ear is developed by constant listening-and-fitting against songs (Ear development by progression-building); and standard chord books are the prescribed external source for changes (Standard chord books resource).

## Chapter 7: Chords with Two Names, Intros, Bop Blues, Endings and Sequences (Lessons 19-23)

# Chapter 7 — Chords with Two Names, Intros, Bop Blues, Endings and Sequences (Lessons 19-23)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

This chapter (Lessons 19-23) teaches the relative-substitution principle — that one chord shape carries two functional names (Gma6 = Emi7, Ama6 = Fmi7) and must be internalized under both identities in all keys (Chords with two names), drilled chromatically up the fingerboard in a workbook (Workbook chromatic drill). Baker pivots the pedagogy here from spoon-fed diagrams to numbered-position recall (Diagram drop-off) and documents the load-bearing tritone-substitution insight that G7#5 and Db9b5 are equivalent root-omitted voicings (Root-omitted voicings), the mechanism behind his b5 substitutions. The chapter then introduces the bop blues reharmonization template in G major (Bop Blues in G major) and culminates in eleven canonical chord-progression sequences framed as the standard harmonic skeletons of the repertoire (Eleven sequences as standards), to be transposed through F, G, Ab, Bb, C, Db, and Eb (Sequence transposition assignment). The characteristic Baker move on display is replacing each standard change with a two-voicing ii-V or relative-minor pair (e.g. Gmi7 Gmi6 for C7, Dmi7 G13b9 for G7 — Cycle No. 1 over standard changes), and the chapter explicitly flags these sequences as the substrate for the later run-connection and solo lessons (Sequences as transposing aid), making it the harmonic spine of the book.

## Chapter 8: Part Two Opens: Time Values, Wrist Development and Scales (Lessons 24-26)

# Chapter 8 — Part Two Opens: Time Values, Wrist Development and Scales (Lessons 24-26)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

This chapter opens Part Two (Lessons 24-26) and pivots from chord study to single-note technique, framing Lessons 24-30 as a set of 28 exercises that constitute the lifelong mechanical foundation for all solo work (28 exercises as key to solo work). Baker establishes the load-bearing technique rules that govern the rest of the book: practice every exercise as slowly as possible and only raise the tempo when relaxed (Slow practice rule); use up-and-down alternate picking on everything from this point forward (Up-and-down stroke mandate); and drill each exercise in two picking-orientation passes — down-up first, then up-down (Picking sequence drill). He pairs the mechanical regime with a non-negotiable ear-training routine — playing along with records, stealing horn solos, strumming chords and humming ideas (Ear development practice) — and insists that timing is developed socially at jam sessions rather than alone (Jam sessions for timing), with a concrete two-hours-per-lesson daily quantum (Two hours per lesson). The chapter also defines the symbol legend X (open string), S (string), and F (fingering) used across every Part Two diagram (Notation key for tablature) and requires the student to memorize each exercise's name end-to-end on the neck so the runs function later as a callable named vocabulary (Memorize exercise names).

## Chapter 9: Scale Runs, Minor/Dominant/Diminished Runs and Blues Pattern (Lessons 27-31)

# Chapter 9 — Scale Runs, Minor/Dominant/Diminished Runs and Blues Pattern (Lessons 27-31)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

This chapter (Lessons 27-31) drills the major-scale, minor, dominant-7th/9th, and diminished runs and then turns them onto a blues pattern, hinging on the load-bearing position-system rule that a single fingering shape is transposed by simple position-shift up and down the neck (Chromatic fingering invariance; Same fingering all directions) — the mechanical key to converting one learned pattern into a 12-key vocabulary at tempo. Baker gates entry into the blues lessons on being able to locate any named run anywhere on the neck on demand (Mastery prerequisite for blues), then declares blues the entry vehicle for all solo work in the method (Blues centrality in jazz). The transposition target is narrowed from all keys to the five horn-friendly blues keys F, G, Ab, Bb, and C (Five blues keys), and the characteristic Baker move is made explicit: the blues solos are built from the exact runs of Lessons 27-30 with no modification, so the exercise vocabulary directly becomes the improvisational material (Reuse of exercise runs).

## Chapter 10: Review, Major/Minor/Dominant Runs, Run Connections and Blues Solos (Lessons 32-37)

# Chapter 10 — Review, Major/Minor/Dominant Runs, Run Connections and Blues Solos (Lessons 32-37)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

This chapter (Lessons 32-37) extends the chords-with-two-names principle from Lesson 19 into the solo domain, establishing the central improvisational license of Baker's method: a minor-7 run substitutes freely for its relative major chord, because the same relative rule that applies to chords applies to runs (Relative run substitution), and the iii-min7 / I-maj7 pair (Bmi7 for Gma7) is added to the toolkit (Close relatives in solos). Baker plants a V7/IV7 substitution at bars 9-10 of the blues so that ii-min7 runs (Ami7 over D7, Gmi7 over C7) sit on those bars (ii-V over bars 9-10) and codifies the always-alter-the-V-into-I voice-leading rule that bridges his chord subs to his run choices (Altered dominant principle), with the workhorse Ami7 to Cmi7 to G major recipe over V to I as the template (Substituting minor-7 runs over V). The characteristic moves generalize beyond blues to ballads and bounce tunes (Universal application), and the chapter operationalizes the leap from imitation to invention with quantified drills: write at least three new solos for each blues example (Make up new solos drill), strum-and-hum the runs against the changes (Hum runs against chords), vary tempo and timing to generate fresh material (Tempo and timing variation), and transpose Lesson 36 across six keys at one day per key while keeping identical fingering and only changing position between the 7th and 12th frets (Transpose blues solos; Blues solo position). Load-bearing rules surfaced: fill-in scaffolded improvisation where blank bars carry a labeled run-type (Fill-in solo method); the riff run is flagged as a deferred category outside the chord-derived system (Riff runs deferred); and the over-arching aesthetic is blending, not throwing runs together (Blend over throw-together).

## Chapter 11: Riffs, Back to Vamp, Bridge Solo and Rhythm Changes (Lessons 38-43)

# Chapter 11 — Riffs, Back to Vamp, Bridge Solo and Rhythm Changes (Lessons 38-43)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 teaches improvisation over vamps and rhythm changes by introducing the riff as the primary melodic unit, defined as 'ideas put together and forced against the chord' that need not be diatonic (Definition of riff runs, Riffs over G major chords). Baker justifies riffs structurally — vamp changes move too fast for chord-tone runs (Why riffs replace chord runs in vamps) — and supplies a substitution rule licensing minor material over dominants (Substituting minor for dominant) plus a mandate that every run be picked with alternating up-and-down strokes (Up-and-down stroke mandate). The chapter installs Baker's load-bearing position vocabulary: position is named by the fret behind which the first finger falls (Defining position by first finger), each key has two governing positions (Two key positions per key) with C major as the lone one-position exception (C major position exception), and fixed positions anchor solo work the same way they anchor chords (Set positions for solo work). Baker's characteristic move surfaces in his internalization rule — riffs must be sung against the chords until they are heard before they are played (Internalize riffs by singing) — and in his pragmatic adaptation rule that dense runs should be thinned and slowed rather than abandoned (Simplify runs that are too dense). The chapter closes by defining 'rhythm changes' as functionally equivalent to vamps but conventionally in B-flat concert (Rhythm changes definition).

## Chapter 12: Reviews, Solo Sketches, Riffs and Groove Riffs (Lessons 44-48)

# Chapter 12 — Reviews, Solo Sketches, Riffs and Groove Riffs (Lessons 44-48)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

Chapter 12 consolidates the riff-and-position machinery from Chapter 11 through review solos, sketches, and a new concept — the groove riff. Baker prescribes transposition-by-writing as a reading-building drill (Writing out builds reading) and directs the student to study run-to-run transitions explicitly (Analyze run connections), reinforcing the earlier rule that riffs may be forced against foreign progressions (Forcing riffs against foreign changes) and re-prescribing the sung-against-the-chord verification step (Sing riffs over progressions). The chapter extends Baker's position framework by introducing an additional 3rd position spanning the 3rd–6th frets (New 3rd position for solos, Range of the new position) with explicit key applicability (A-flat, B-flat, C, D-flat, E-flat major, marginally usable for F and G). The load-bearing new term is the groove riff, defined as a riff whose first and last courses match and which is repeated consecutively (Groove riff definition); its use is scoped to blues and vamp progressions, conventionally placed at the start and end of a song (Where to use groove riffs), and operationalized as the same run repeated across the first and last 12 bars of a blues (Repetition over 12 bars). Baker's characteristic move is the strict position-discipline he then imposes: the entire groove-riff solo must be executed without leaving the 3rd position (Solo entirely in 3rd position).

## Chapter 13: Building Around the Melody and Solo Patterns (Lessons 49-52)

# Chapter 13 — Building Around the Melody and Solo Patterns (Lessons 49-52)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Chapter 13 teaches two complementary approaches to constructing solos: building around a stated melody, and building from a fixed rhythmic template called a solo pattern. The melody-building method is governed by an aesthetic rule — the listener should still hear the tune even when it is not literally present (Implied-melody principle) — and operationalized by running chords around the melody until ideas emerge (Build by running chords around melody), all executed under a strict position lock between the 7th and 11th frets (Position locked at 7th fret). The chapter also offers a concrete creative procedure for inventing groove riffs by singing candidate riffs against a 12-bar blues until one fits three times through (Finding groove riffs by singing), tying back to Chapter 12's groove-riff concept. The load-bearing new term is the solo pattern, defined as a per-course rhythmic frame held constant throughout the solo (Solo pattern definition), demonstrated by identical timing across bars with different pitches (Same timing, different notes) and distilled to the generative principle that the first measure sets the pattern for everything that follows (First measure sets the pattern). Baker's characteristic moves are visible in his framing of the chapter's worked examples as a permanent pattern library keyed to progression type (Patterns library for future reference) and in the meta-rule that closes the chapter — repeated review of earlier lessons is itself a learning mechanism, since each pass reveals something new (Review to keep learning).
CHAPTER SUMMARIES>>>
